text: The surgeon uses near-infrared fluorescence with indocyanine green to visualize and identify the ureter during laparoscopic anterior resection.
Indocyanin green is injected retrogradely into the catheter, visibly filling the left ureter with fluorescent dye.
The surgeon uses the Davinci XI system to switch between standard white light and near-infrared views, enhancing the visibility of anatomical structures during laparoscopic anterior resection.
The ureter is visible as a fluorescent green signal due to the indocyanin green filling, allowing easy identification of its position during the laparoscopic anterior resection.

The catheter is positioned externally and connected to a syringe, enabling the surgeon to boost the indocyanine green signal if it fades, and facilitating easy removal at the end of the procedure to minimize patient discomfort.

The da Vinci XI surgical system is shown utilizing its Firefly capability to switch between standard white light and near-infrared illumination, enhancing the visibility and safety of critical structures during the procedure.
